MIKIA Kenya

Activities
33314415-10e1-81e4-9ba3-f33b9df57eee, 33314415-10e1-816c-ba20-fd58db6ba1ee, 33314415-10e1-8123-86a5-d96670472c70
type
organization
location_lat
1.5
background_section
## Background Northern Kenya’s Maasai pastoralists have long moved with seasonal grass, water, and wildlife—knowledge and practice tuned to semi-arid rangelands. That social–ecological system is under strain. Climate change brings deeper droughts, erratic rains, and degraded range. Mobility in search of pasture raises friction with other users, access barriers, and safety risks. Conservation fencing has sliced migration routes; elephants and people are squeezed into tighter spaces, inflating conflict. Meanwhile, expert-led fixes sometimes sideline intergenerational methods that treat people, livestock, and wildlife as one connected landscape. ### Origins and Network **My Indigenous Knowledge in Action** ([MIKIA](https://youtu.be/RhXCT1toHIs)) takes its name from a Maasai phrase meaning, roughly, **“don’t take me away”**—a plea to keep Indigenous knowledge present. Transmission practices are themselves threatened; if they erode, so does the chain from elder to child. MIKIA documents, safeguards, and revives knowledge through intergenerational exchange and deliberate pairing of Indigenous and scientific insight. Founder **Ramson Karmushu** works from within Maasai communities in Northern Kenya toward land use that keeps biodiversity and livelihoods viable across coming decades.
patterns

Lake Turkana Academy-Kalokol, Isiolo, Kenya

Northern Kenya

temp
improper location but good for now?
description
My Indigenous Knowledge in Action (MIKIA) works to document, preserve, and regenerate indigenous knowledge in Northern Kenya, particularly among Maasai pastoralist communities. Through intergenerational knowledge transfer, integration with scientific knowledge, and community-led initiatives, MIKIA supports climate resilience, biodiversity conservation, and indigenous self-determination.
id
mikia-kenya
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy MIKIA’s programme mix—centres, maps, and intergenerational curricula—treats Indigenous knowledge as something to **keep**, not extract or replace. The horizon is stronger self-determination for Maasai institutions and lifeways, with customary and scientific knowledges held in deliberate conversation rather than substitution.
website
https://youtu.be/RhXCT1toHIs
bioregional_context
Northern Kenya encompasses vast pastoralist landscapes where indigenous Maasai communities have lived for generations. This region faces severe climate change impacts, with communities experiencing increasing droughts, changing rainfall patterns, and ecosystem degradation. The landscapes are interconnected across national borders, with traditional migration routes and wildlife corridors that have been disrupted by conservation fencing and development.
category
Bioregional Organizer
location_lng
